evidence.claim_text: The increased oxida-tion resistance conferred by 6-8% chromium to an 80-20 Ni-Mo alloy is shown in Figure 38A. Also shown in Figure 38B is a list of a number of alloys melted in the course of developing the Hastelloy N (INOR-8). ND/74/66 ISSUE 1 DATE 12/16/74 In related developmental studies, Manley and coworkers28 reported that while chromium in the newly developed alloy was undesirable because of fused fluoride corrosion, the addition of at least 6% chromium was required for oxidation resistance for long time service at temperatures above 650°C (l200°F). A comprehensive review of the work conducted by ORNL and other participating laboratories which led to the 29 development of the Hastelloy N alloy was published in 1969 by H. CHARGE NO. McCoy.The results of corrosion tests obtained by ORNL in continuing studies involving the behavior of Hastelloy N and other materials subjected to various types of fuel, blanket, fertile fissile and coolant molten salts for various exposure times, temperatures, velocities, Δt's in natural thermal convection loops (NCL) and forced circulating loops (FCL) are described in referenced ORNL MSR semiannual progress reports 30-38 and further discussion is beyond the scope of present study.Table 23 shows the status of the MSR program thermal convection loops (NCL) as of 8/31/72.Summarily the results of the continuing investigation indicate that the Hastelloy N alloy is the most suitable material for high temperature molten salt service having average corrosion rates of several tenths of a mil/yr.The corrosion rate of the Hastelloy N material is increased when the coolant salt (NaBF4-NaF, 92-8 mole %) is contaminated by the presence of impurities particularly oxygen and moisture which results in the formation of HF. However, analytical techniques are being used to determine and monitor the oxygen and water impurity levels while other techniques are being developed to remove these impurities from the coolant salt. BY APPROVED PAGE 6-24 8-25-2431 DOCUMENT NO.

evidence.claim_text: The corrosion of various materials, particularly Inconel and INOR-8, by fused salt mixtures in the temperature range 1100 to 1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F} is being investigated with forced-circulation loops in which high thermal gradients can be attained in order to simulate reactor operating conditions. Low corrosion rates are anticipated for Inconel and INOR-8 in contact with the fused salts of interest in the MSR program, and thus long-time effects are to be investigated.

evidence.claim_text: A loop operated previously with this mixture at 1250^{\circ}\mathrm{F} showed approximately 1 mil of attack over a similar test period. No attack was evident in any of the INOR-8 loops even after operation, in one case, for 3114 hr. An Inconel loop which operated 1000 hr at 1350^{\circ}\mathrm{F} with the mixture LiF- \mathsf{BeF}_2\mathsf{-UF}_4 (62-37-1 mole %) was attacked to a depth of 3 mils. Operation of an Inconel loop at 1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F} for 4360 hr with the mixture NaF-LiF-KF (11.5-46.5-42 mole %) resulted in the formation of subsurface voids to a depth of 2 mils. Two Inconel and four INOR-8 thermal-convection loops, which circulated various fused-fluoride-salt mixtures, were examined.

evidence.claim_text: Metallography of the INOR-8 specimens removed from the second loop revealed heavy surface roughening and pitting, but, like the first loop, specimens of the as-received tubing revealed a similar pitted condition. Examination of the INOR-8 spacers, which held the molybdenum in contact with the graphite, exhibited attack in the form of surface roughening and pitting to a maximum depth of 1 mil along the inner surface which had been exposed to salt. Evidence of scattered carburized areas to a maximum depth of 5 mils was found on the outer surfaces of the spacers. A surface layer, similar to that generally present after long-term INOR-8 tests, was found along the outer surfaces which were in contact with the molybdenum. The maximum depth of this layer was 1/4 mil. These carburized spots corresponded to areas where the INOR-8 was in direct contact with the graphite.

evidence.claim_text: 51) and have demonstrated the ability to absorb 160\% shear strain without observable internal cracking or loss of integrity. Although there is no experimental evidence beyond 2 \times 10^{22} neutrons/ \mathrm{cm}^2 , several factors lead to optimism about the ability of graphite to sustain much greater damage. The first factor is the recognition that in the 1292^{\circ}\mathrm{F} temperature range the contractual dimensional changes for the first 10^{22} neutrons/ \mathrm{cm}^2 are due to the repair of the damage caused by cooling the graphite from its graphitization temperature. Doses above 10^{22} neutrons/ \mathrm{cm}^2 produce an expansion in the "c" direction and a contraction in the "a" direction. Even so, measurements obtained from irradiated pyrolytic graphites indicate that a deformation rate of about 1\% shear strain per 10^{21} neutrons/ \mathrm{cm}^2 must be absorbed. Thus, for a 5-year life, the graphite tubes operating in a flux region of 6 \times 10^{14} neutrons \mathrm{cm}^{-2} sec ^{-1} must absorb an internal deformation of about 100\% shear strain. Very fine crystallite pyrocarbons have been irradiated under conditions producing crystallite shearing rates of 60\% per 10^{22} neutrons/ \mathrm{cm}^2 (ref.

evidence.claim_text: Graphs illustrating the relative corrosion resistance of various alloys in a LIF-BeF2-THF4-UF4 molten salt are shown in Figure 39. 6.4.3.3CORROSION RESISTANCE TO SUPERCRITICAL STEAMThe best commercial alloy found for the supercritical steam service is Incoloy 800. A graph illustrating manufacturers data on the relative strengths of Inconel 600, Incoloy 800 and Inconel 625 alloys are shown for comparison purposes in Figure 40. 6.4.4 CRITICAL REVIEW OF HASTELLOY N AND DUPEX TUBING 6.4.4.1 HASTELLOY N The dilemma concerning the use of Hastelloy N in the MSBR application is largely based on the fact that it has no prior commercial use in steam generating applications and that the present nuclear application requires the use of tubing that will be subjected to steam side service conditions substantially more severe than those previously encountered in conventional power plant operations. The recent work published by J. This alloy when exposed to superheated steam at temperatures of 1050°-1100°F forms a tenacious uniform and predictable oxide scale and possesses high temperature strength, ductility, creep resistance and thermal stability required for long term high temperature/pressure steam service.The alloy has been widely used for commercial steam generating applications particularly where increased strength and resistance to stress corrosion cracking processes are required. The maximum allowable stresses shown for the Incoloy 800 alloy in Table 25 were established and reviewed by the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code Committee and the stress values approved for use on 4/29/74 under Code Case 1592 for Nuclear Vessels. BY APPROVED PAGE 6-25 CHARGE NO. ND/74/66 ISSUE 1 DATE 12/16/74 Also shown in Table 24 for comparison purposes, are the maximum allowable design stresses for 304 SS, 316 SS and 2 1/4Cr-1 Mo alloys. Hammond et al39 indicates that the surface condition and prior heat treatment has a pronounced effect upon the stress corrosion cracking proclivity of welded and non welded U-bend specimens subjected to impure, oxygenated steam environments containing small quantities of NaCl introduced during cyclic operation from saturated temperatures @ 544°F to superheat temperatures of 800-900°F. A photograph of the U-bend specimen holder with mounted specimens and two schematics illustrating specimens/environmental conditions during cyclic testing and the high pressure chloride SCC facility at the Bartow Florida Plant are shown in Figures 41 A, B, C. In these tests Hammond observed that the ground specimen surface condition promoted the highest susceptibility to cracking in a number of alloys including Hastelloy N while none of the ground and properly annealed Hastelloy N specimens failed in the severe test environment. BY APPROVED PAGE 6-26 8-25-2431 DOCUMENT NO.

evidence.claim_text: Inconel and types-430, -304, and -321 stainless steel have been tested for 100 hr at 538, 704, and 1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C} . The results of previous tests of these materials at 816^{\circ}\mathrm{C} are also included for comparison. The temperature range for sensitization is usually accepted as 400 to 850^{\circ}\mathrm{C} . Temperature dependence tests in NaF-KF-LiF-UF _4 (10.9-43.5-44.5-1.1 mole %) have been completed. The maximum penetrations of both containers and specimens are listed in Table 10. Apparently the phenomenon of sensitization occurring in type-304 stainless steel and Inconel has little if any effect on corrosion by these fluoride mixtures under the testing conditions employed.

evidence.claim_text: Each of the eight specimens was contained in a capsule machined from the same material as the specimens, with the exception of the specimens of type 305 stainless steel which was contained in tubing of type 304 stainless steel. The specimens were tested in NaF-KF-LiF- \mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_{4} (10.9-43.5-44.5-1.1 mole %) for 100 hr at 816^{\circ}C. The compositions of the steels and results of the tests are shown in Table 6.3. Two specimens were commercial stainless steel, types 304 and 305, while the other six specimens were stainless steels cast and extruded at MIT. Three of these special steels contained 18\% chromium and 12\% nickel, while the other three contained 18\% chromium and 8\% nickel.

evidence.claim_text: In three other supposedly duplicate loops operated for 1000 hr, attack to depths of 8, 13, and 13.5 mils developed. Two supposedly duplicate Inconel loops were operated for 500 hr with the \mathsf{UF}_3\text{-}\mathsf{UF}_4 mixture in NaF-KF-LiF (11.5-42-46.5 mole %) at a hot-leg temperature of 1500^{\circ}F ( \Delta T of \sim 200^{\circ}F ); however, the rough surface of one loop showed maximum penetration to a depth of 2 mils, whereas the other loop was attacked to a depth of 13 mils.

evidence.claim_text: Long duration tests of Inconel forced-circulation loops were made with both NaK and sodium circulated at a maximum fluid temperature of 1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F} . The test with NaK, for which a temperature gradient of 600^{\circ}\mathrm{F} was used, was terminated after 2760 hr. The loop operated with sodium for 4000 hr with a temperature gradient of 800^{\circ}\mathrm{F} showed considerably larger deposits that reached a thickness of 50 mils in some areas. The cooled section of this loop was found to contain extensive crystalline deposits that ranged to 14 mils in thickness.
evidence.title: AIRCRAFT NUCLEAR PROPULSION PROJECT

evidence.claim_text: A specimen of type-304 stainless steel that had been electroplated with approximately 8 mils of nickel was lightly cut along one face with a hacksaw in order to expose the base metal. There was a layer approximately 12 mils thick of either corrosion product or unremoved fluoride, beneath which was a region of severe intergranular attack 15 mils deep (Fig. This specimen was then tested in NaF-KF-LiF \mathbf{UF}_4 (10.9-43.5-44.5-1.1 mole %) for 100 hr at 816^{\circ}\mathrm{C} under vacuum. At the sawcut the type-304 stainless steel was more severely attacked than any other steel corrosion specimen that had been tested in the static fluoride mixtures.
evidence.title: ASSIFIED

evidence.claim_text: Graphs illustrating the relative corrosion resistance of various alloys in a LIF-BeF2-THF4-UF4 molten salt are shown in Figure 39. 6.4.3.3CORROSION RESISTANCE TO SUPERCRITICAL STEAMThe best commercial alloy found for the supercritical steam service is Incoloy 800. A graph illustrating manufacturers data on the relative strengths of Inconel 600, Incoloy 800 and Inconel 625 alloys are shown for comparison purposes in Figure 40. 6.4.4 CRITICAL REVIEW OF HASTELLOY N AND DUPEX TUBING 6.4.4.1 HASTELLOY N The dilemma concerning the use of Hastelloy N in the MSBR application is largely based on the fact that it has no prior commercial use in steam generating applications and that the present nuclear application requires the use of tubing that will be subjected to steam side service conditions substantially more severe than those previously encountered in conventional power plant operations. The recent work published by J. This alloy when exposed to superheated steam at temperatures of 1050°-1100°F forms a tenacious uniform and predictable oxide scale and possesses high temperature strength, ductility, creep resistance and thermal stability required for long term high temperature/pressure steam service.The alloy has been widely used for commercial steam generating applications particularly where increased strength and resistance to stress corrosion cracking processes are required. The maximum allowable stresses shown for the Incoloy 800 alloy in Table 25 were established and reviewed by the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code Committee and the stress values approved for use on 4/29/74 under Code Case 1592 for Nuclear Vessels. BY APPROVED PAGE 6-25 CHARGE NO. ND/74/66 ISSUE 1 DATE 12/16/74 Also shown in Table 24 for comparison purposes, are the maximum allowable design stresses for 304 SS, 316 SS and 2 1/4Cr-1 Mo alloys. Hammond et al39 indicates that the surface condition and prior heat treatment has a pronounced effect upon the stress corrosion cracking proclivity of welded and non welded U-bend specimens subjected to impure, oxygenated steam environments containing small quantities of NaCl introduced during cyclic operation from saturated temperatures @ 544°F to superheat temperatures of 800-900°F. A photograph of the U-bend specimen holder with mounted specimens and two schematics illustrating specimens/environmental conditions during cyclic testing and the high pressure chloride SCC facility at the Bartow Florida Plant are shown in Figures 41 A, B, C. In these tests Hammond observed that the ground specimen surface condition promoted the highest susceptibility to cracking in a number of alloys including Hastelloy N while none of the ground and properly annealed Hastelloy N specimens failed in the severe test environment. BY APPROVED PAGE 6-26 8-25-2431 DOCUMENT NO.

evidence.claim_text: Loop 1258, constructed of type 304L stainless steel (11.07% Ni, 18.31% Cr, 0.028% C, bal Fe) and containing removable corrosion specimens in the hot leg, operated nine years (80,000 hr) and thus provided long-term stainless steel-fuel salt compatibility data. An LiF-BeF _2 -ZrF _4 -UF _4 -ThF _4 (70-23-5-1-1 mole %) salt circulated in the loop at a maximum temperature of 688^{\circ}\mathrm{C} and a minimum temperature of 588^{\circ}\mathrm{C} .

evidence.claim_text: One loop is constructed of Hastelloy N and has operated satisfactorily at 1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F} for 47,440 hr. The loop has operated at 1250^{\circ}\mathrm{F} for 36,160 hr, and the removable specimens have indicated a corrosion rate of 2 mils/year. One loop, constructed of Croloy 9M, plugged in 1440 hr because of mass transfer and the deposition of iron crystals in the Two loops have also been run using NaF-KF-BF 3 , which is a possible coolant salt.

evidence.claim_text: We have deposited coatings of tungsten on plain carbon steels and type 304 stainless steel in an effort to obtain materials that have good corrosion resistance in bismuth.
evidence.title: ORNL-4449

value_text: We have deposited coatings of tungsten on plain carbon steels and type 304 stainless steel in an effort to obtain materials that have good corrosion resistance in bismuth.

evidence.claim_text: Molybdenum and niobium appear to be resistant to molten fluoride salts at temperatures to 816^{\circ}\mathrm{C} ( 1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F} ). Vanadium and tantalum were attached to a depth of 1 mil in 100 hours at these temperatures. At 700^{\circ}\mathrm{C} gold and rhodium resisted attack by a mixture of sodium and uranium fluoride; platinum was slightly attacked, and copper was rapidly attacked.

evidence.claim_text: The metallurgical evaluation showed that the attack by the fluoroborate salt drastically changed the composition and properties of the type 304 stainless steel probe during service. The highly attacked region had a final composition near that of 78 Permalloy (Ni-22% Fe), a highly ferromagnetic material.

Table 5 summarizes recent data ^{9,10} obtained from natural-circulation loop tests and compares the compatibilities of some fluoride salts with type 304 stainless steel and with Hastelloy N.

evidence.claim_text: Corrosion of type 304L stainless steel and Hastelloy N was studied in gaseous mixtures of \mathrm{BF}_3 , air, and argon at 600, 300, and 200^{\circ}\mathrm{C} . At 600^{\circ}\mathrm{C} under air, specimens of both alloys were completely destroyed in both fuel salt and fluoroborate salt mixtures. With no salt immersion, the various gases produced very small changes in the alloys at 200^{\circ}\mathrm{C} . Also with no salt, only small changes were noted at 300 and 600^{\circ}\mathrm{C} for Hastelloy N in any gas and for the stainless steel in air. At 600^{\circ}\mathrm{C} , large quantities of chromium and iron oxides were produced on the stainless steel by the air- \mathrm{BF}_3 mixture and large amounts of unidentified corrosion products by \mathrm{BF}_3 , argon- \mathrm{BF}_3 , and argon-air.

evidence.claim_text: Static tests of various combinations of metals in fluorides at 1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F} have shown that one of the metals is attacked to a greater extent than the other, even though the heavily attacked metal may be relatively unattacked when alone in the fluoride. The fluoride fuel NaF-ZrF _4 -UF _4 (50-46-4 mole%) has also been circulated in nickel and type 430 stainless steel loops for 500 hr at 1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F} ; no plugging occurred, but some mass transfer was observed in the stainless steel loop. The attack by fluorides on Inconel at 1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F} apparently increases in intensity, although not in maximum depth, as the \mathsf{UF}_4 content of the melt is increased from 0 to 6.5 mole%. Of the metals tested, oxidized types 347 and 304 stainless steel and molybdenum-nickel alloy produced the least mass transfer.

evidence.claim_text: An 80\% Mg-20% Li alloy being considered as a crew-compartment shielding material was corrosion tested in water at various temperatures; the weight losses were found to be negligible. In an effort to find a brazing alloy that has good resistance to sodium and to \mathsf{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4} tests of several brazes on type 304 stainless steel T-joints were made. Similar joints of A-nickel brazed with various alloys were also tested in \mathsf{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4} at 1500^{\circ}\mathsf{F} and in sodium hydroxide at 1100 and 1500^{\circ}\mathsf{F} . Additional screening tests were made of carbides of boron, titanium, zirconium, and chromium in static sodium, lithium, and \mathsf{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4} at 1500^{\circ}\mathsf{F} for 100-hr periods. Studies of the corrosion of Inconel, type 316 stainless steel, Hastelloy B, and several special alloys were continued through the use of thermal-convection apparatus.
evidence.title: AIRCRAFT NUCLEAR PROPULSION PROJECT

evidence.claim_text: Several type 304 stainless steel T-joints brazed with experimental alloys prepared by the Wall Colmonoy Corporation were tested in sodium and in \mathrm{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4} . Most of the alloys had good resistance to the fluoride mixture, but only the 10.2\% P- 13\% Cr- 76.8\% Ni alloy had good resistance in both sodium and the fluoride mixture. All the alloys tested had good resistance in the fluoride mixture, except the 69\% Ni- 20\% Cr- 11\% Si alloy, whereas all the alloys had poor resistance to sodium hydroxide, except the 82\% Au- 18\% Ni alloy.

evidence.claim_text: In a test of type 1043 mild steel (0.433\%) in static sodium at 1830^{\circ}\mathrm{F} for 100 hr, significant decarburization occurred. The carbon content of the mild-steel specimens decreased, and the carbon content of the capsule walls in contact with the sodium increased; the two types of capsules used were Armco iron (0.019\%) C) and type 304 ELC stainless steel (0.022\%) C). Two special heats of Stellarite were exposed to static lithium at 1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F} for 100 hr. They were attacked to a depth of 2 to 3 mils, in general, but there were isolated areas that suffered very heavy attack.

The loop operated for 240 hr before failing because of plug formation; the hot- and cold-leg temperatures were 815 and 500^{\circ}\mathrm{C} . In previous loops with type 347 stainless steel inserts for which the standard deoxidized procedure was followed, plugging occurred in approximately 150 hours.

evidence.claim_text: Graphs illustrating the relative corrosion resistance of various alloys in a LIF-BeF2-THF4-UF4 molten salt are shown in Figure 39. 6.4.3.3CORROSION RESISTANCE TO SUPERCRITICAL STEAMThe best commercial alloy found for the supercritical steam service is Incoloy 800. A graph illustrating manufacturers data on the relative strengths of Inconel 600, Incoloy 800 and Inconel 625 alloys are shown for comparison purposes in Figure 40. 6.4.4 CRITICAL REVIEW OF HASTELLOY N AND DUPEX TUBING 6.4.4.1 HASTELLOY N The dilemma concerning the use of Hastelloy N in the MSBR application is largely based on the fact that it has no prior commercial use in steam generating applications and that the present nuclear application requires the use of tubing that will be subjected to steam side service conditions substantially more severe than those previously encountered in conventional power plant operations. The recent work published by J. This alloy when exposed to superheated steam at temperatures of 1050°-1100°F forms a tenacious uniform and predictable oxide scale and possesses high temperature strength, ductility, creep resistance and thermal stability required for long term high temperature/pressure steam service.The alloy has been widely used for commercial steam generating applications particularly where increased strength and resistance to stress corrosion cracking processes are required. The maximum allowable stresses shown for the Incoloy 800 alloy in Table 25 were established and reviewed by the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code Committee and the stress values approved for use on 4/29/74 under Code Case 1592 for Nuclear Vessels. BY APPROVED PAGE 6-25 CHARGE NO. ND/74/66 ISSUE 1 DATE 12/16/74 Also shown in Table 24 for comparison purposes, are the maximum allowable design stresses for 304 SS, 316 SS and 2 1/4Cr-1 Mo alloys. Hammond et al39 indicates that the surface condition and prior heat treatment has a pronounced effect upon the stress corrosion cracking proclivity of welded and non welded U-bend specimens subjected to impure, oxygenated steam environments containing small quantities of NaCl introduced during cyclic operation from saturated temperatures @ 544°F to superheat temperatures of 800-900°F. A photograph of the U-bend specimen holder with mounted specimens and two schematics illustrating specimens/environmental conditions during cyclic testing and the high pressure chloride SCC facility at the Bartow Florida Plant are shown in Figures 41 A, B, C. In these tests Hammond observed that the ground specimen surface condition promoted the highest susceptibility to cracking in a number of alloys including Hastelloy N while none of the ground and properly annealed Hastelloy N specimens failed in the severe test environment. BY APPROVED PAGE 6-26 8-25-2431 DOCUMENT NO.

evidence.claim_text: Parallel tests have shown that exposure for 72 hr raised the chromium and nickel values slightly (40 to 400 ppm) without perceptible increase in iron concentration. There is some indication that the values for nickel and chromium are slightly higher when filtration is performed at 600 rather than 800^{\circ}\mathrm{C} . When metallic iron is exposed to the eutectic mixture at 800^{\circ}\mathrm{C} , 1500 to 2500 ppm of iron appears in the filtrate. When chromium powder is used, 150 to 450 ppm of the element is solubilized. Previous examination ^{(15)} indicated that little (20 to 100 ppm) chromium and nickel and large amounts (1500 to 3000 ppm) of iron were solubilized by a 6-hr treatment of type 316 stainless steel with the NaF-KF-LiF eutectic at 800^{\circ}\mathrm{C} . It was reported previously that when the NaF-KF-LiF eutectic contained added \mathrm{UF_4} , the iron content of the filtrate rose to 6000 to 7000 ppm and the amount of soluble chromium increased to 500 to 2000 ppm.

evidence.claim_text: A type 316 stainless steel loop (No. The cold leg of this loop was held above 1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F} by using a hot leg temperature of 1630^{\circ}\mathrm{F} and wrapping the cold leg with asbestos tape. No sign of plugging was evident at 500 hours. The loop then plugged after 366 hr at the lower temperature, or a total of 866 hours. 117) circulated NaF-KF-LiF-UF _4 (10.9-43.5-44.5-1.1 mole %) for 500 hours. This is only the second type 316 stainless steel loop that has not plugged, and both of these loops were operated under similar conditions. At this time, the hot leg temperature was reduced to 1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F} , and the asbestos tape was removed so that the loop would circulate under standard conditions. The fluoride in the sections of this loop melted at below 1250^{\circ}\mathrm{F} , and it all melted.

evidence.claim_text: Two type 316 stainless steel thermal convection loops operated for 500 hr at 1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F} with \mathsf{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4} (50-46-4 mole %) without plugging. The attack found in these loops appears to be much more comparable to that found with Inconel than was the attack in the loops that plugged with the fluoride NaF-KF-LiF-UF _4 (10.9-43.5-44.5-1.1 mole %). These were the first loops of stainless steel to circulate uranium-bearing fluorides at 1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F} without plugging. In loop 133 the depth of attack was 5 to 11 mils, while in loop 134 it was 4 to 7 mils.

evidence.claim_text: Both these loops also showed mass transfer, but not so much as was found in loop 4689-4. Loop 4689-6 had a type 316 stainless steel economizer and cold leg, while loop 4689-5 had only a type 316 stainless steel cold leg. The hot legs showed similar intergranular attack to a depth of 2.5 mils in loop 4689-5 and 1.5 mils in loop 4689-6. These data indicate that a type 316 stainless steel cold leg reduces the mass transfer slightly, but further study of the variables in the process is necessary. Two additional loops were operated with portions of the cold legs constructed from type 316 stainless steel. The maximum deposit thickness in loop 4689-5 was 15 mils and in loop 4689-6 it was 12 mils.

evidence.claim_text: Figure 10.13 shows the microstructure of the hottest and coldest specimens. Loop NCL-22 was constructed of type 316 stainless steel and operated for 4491\mathrm{hr} prior to termination. After 2842\mathrm{hr} , the maximum weight loss was 6.2\mathrm{mg/cm}^2 (0.96 mil/year). In the next 645-hr period, the maximum corrosion rate was 0.83\mathrm{mil/year} , but in the final 811-hr period it was >1\mathrm{mil/year} . The average uniform corrosion rate for the hottest specimen was 1\mathrm{mil/year} for the entire 4298 hr of exposure to salt. The maximum depth of attack in the hot leg was approximately 3 mils, and the attack area is probably depleted in chromium.[7]

evidence.claim_text: Figure 33 shows the microstructure of the hottest specimen after 1410 hr. Subsurface voids are seen for a distance of 1 mil. After 2842 hr exposure the specimen at 650^{\circ}\mathrm{C} had lost a total of 6.2\mathrm{mg/cm}^2 , equivalent to a uniform corrosion rate of 0.96 mil/year. Loop 22 was a type 316 stainless steel thermal convection loop which operated for 4491 hr with a \mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ThF_4 - UF_4} salt mixture identified in Table 1. The harp portion of the loop was 1-in.-OD tubing with 0.109-in. wall thickness. The material was from heat X-22662, U.S. National Tube, and the ORNL Inspection Engineering Number was IR-9141, Y-12 No. The nominal composition of the alloy was 16.6\% Cr, 13.6\% Ni, 2.25\% Mo, bal Fe.

evidence.claim_text: The main corrosion reaction in this system is 2\mathrm{UF}_4 + \mathrm{Cr} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CrF}_2 + 2\mathrm{UF}_3 ; thus, in order to determine if we could decrease the mass transfer of the stainless steel, we added \mathrm{UF}_3 to the salt after the 2842-hr exposure measurements to make the salt less oxidizing. In the next 645-hr time period the mass transfer was somewhat less, and the overall corrosion rate was 0.83 mil/year. In the final 811-hr time period, the weight loss of the hottest specimen increased such that the uniform corrosion rate was >1 mil/year.

evidence.claim_text: Graphs illustrating the relative corrosion resistance of various alloys in a LIF-BeF2-THF4-UF4 molten salt are shown in Figure 39. 6.4.3.3CORROSION RESISTANCE TO SUPERCRITICAL STEAMThe best commercial alloy found for the supercritical steam service is Incoloy 800. A graph illustrating manufacturers data on the relative strengths of Inconel 600, Incoloy 800 and Inconel 625 alloys are shown for comparison purposes in Figure 40. 6.4.4 CRITICAL REVIEW OF HASTELLOY N AND DUPEX TUBING 6.4.4.1 HASTELLOY N The dilemma concerning the use of Hastelloy N in the MSBR application is largely based on the fact that it has no prior commercial use in steam generating applications and that the present nuclear application requires the use of tubing that will be subjected to steam side service conditions substantially more severe than those previously encountered in conventional power plant operations. The recent work published by J. This alloy when exposed to superheated steam at temperatures of 1050°-1100°F forms a tenacious uniform and predictable oxide scale and possesses high temperature strength, ductility, creep resistance and thermal stability required for long term high temperature/pressure steam service.The alloy has been widely used for commercial steam generating applications particularly where increased strength and resistance to stress corrosion cracking processes are required. The maximum allowable stresses shown for the Incoloy 800 alloy in Table 25 were established and reviewed by the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code Committee and the stress values approved for use on 4/29/74 under Code Case 1592 for Nuclear Vessels. BY APPROVED PAGE 6-25 CHARGE NO. ND/74/66 ISSUE 1 DATE 12/16/74 Also shown in Table 24 for comparison purposes, are the maximum allowable design stresses for 304 SS, 316 SS and 2 1/4Cr-1 Mo alloys. Hammond et al39 indicates that the surface condition and prior heat treatment has a pronounced effect upon the stress corrosion cracking proclivity of welded and non welded U-bend specimens subjected to impure, oxygenated steam environments containing small quantities of NaCl introduced during cyclic operation from saturated temperatures @ 544°F to superheat temperatures of 800-900°F. A photograph of the U-bend specimen holder with mounted specimens and two schematics illustrating specimens/environmental conditions during cyclic testing and the high pressure chloride SCC facility at the Bartow Florida Plant are shown in Figures 41 A, B, C. In these tests Hammond observed that the ground specimen surface condition promoted the highest susceptibility to cracking in a number of alloys including Hastelloy N while none of the ground and properly annealed Hastelloy N specimens failed in the severe test environment. BY APPROVED PAGE 6-26 8-25-2431 DOCUMENT NO.

In the first 1300\mathrm{hr} of operation, the weight loss of the Te-coated specimen equaled the amount of tellurium that had been added to the specimen. In the next 2100\mathrm{hr} , the weight loss of the Te-coated specimen was the same as the uncoated Hastelloy N specimen in a neighboring position.

evidence.claim_text: After the first 42 hr in the loop, all specimens lost >66\% of their initial {}^{127m}\mathrm{Te} activity. By comparison, a control sample stored in air at room temperature lost 4\% of its {}^{127m}\mathrm{Te} activity. After 159 hr all loop specimens had lost 75\% of their initial activity while the control specimen had lost 8\% . Analyses after 396 and 701 hr indicated that hot leg specimens were continuing to lose {}^{127m}\mathrm{Te} , but that a small increase in activity was found in cold leg specimens. Measurements after 1318 and 1792 hr indicated no further changes in {}^{127m}\mathrm{Te} activity on specimens in either the hot or cold leg. Since the loop operating temperature was above the melting temperature of tellurium, and most of the loss in tellurium occurred during the first 42 hr (the first time period at which measurements were made), the mechanism of tellurium loss into the salt is undetermined.

evidence.claim_text: The effect of the observed deposit was evident in the gradual reduction of the over-all heat transfer coefficient during the 19 days that the loop operated. It is interesting to note that a 10-mil layer of pure iron inside the center tube would have a thermal resistance only one tenth that of the Inconel wall and would make only a 2\% change in the over-all coefficient. This would indicate that the observed deposit had a higher thermal resistance than a 10-mil iron layer. The total reduction in the heat transfer coefficient was 16\% over this period.

evidence.claim_text: Type 316 stainless steel showed somewhat less attack when subjected to the hydrogen-treated material than when exposed to material prepared in the standard fashion. The Inconel tubes were found to be lightly attacked (0.5- to 1-mil penetration) when in contact with the hydrofluorinated material, but they evidenced no attack when exposed to the hydrogen-treated material. A test was run in the tilting furnace under standard conditions of time and temperature with \mathrm{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4} (50-46-4 mole %) that had been treated with hydrogen after purification by the normal hydrogenation-hydrofluorination method.

evidence.claim_text: Two type 316 stainless steel thermal convection loops operated for 500 hr at 1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F} with \mathsf{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4} (50-46-4 mole %) without plugging. The attack found in these loops appears to be much more comparable to that found with Inconel than was the attack in the loops that plugged with the fluoride NaF-KF-LiF-UF _4 (10.9-43.5-44.5-1.1 mole %). These were the first loops of stainless steel to circulate uranium-bearing fluorides at 1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F} without plugging. In loop 133 the depth of attack was 5 to 11 mils, while in loop 134 it was 4 to 7 mils.

evidence.claim_text: While the corrosion rates after 500 hr at 1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F} and at 1650^{\circ}\mathrm{F} are comparable, the initial corrosion rate is higher at the higher temperature. However, high initial corrosion rates (correlated with \mathrm{NiF}_2 and \mathrm{FeF}_2 fuel contaminants) are superseded around 250 hr by much lower corrosion rates, possibly because of the oxidation of chromium by \mathrm{UF}_4 . A type 316 stainless steel insert in the Inconel loop was preferentially attacked by the fluorides, whereas graphite immersed in the fluoride causes carburization of the Inconel loop.

evidence.claim_text: As previously mentioned, (5) no difference in maximum depth of attack was found when loops were operated at 1650^{\circ}\mathrm{F} instead of at 1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F} for 500 hours. After 100 hr of operation, however, the maximum attack in a loop operated at 1650^{\circ}\mathrm{F} was 7 mils, while in a loop operated at 1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F} , it was only 4 mils. After 100 hr at 1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F} , the holes were small and evenly distributed, that is, similar to those found at low temperatures in 500 hr of operation. When the loop was operated at 1650^{\circ}\mathrm{F} , the holes were larger and were concentrated in the grain boundaries.

evidence.claim_text: Loop 379, the second in this series, was operated with a length of nickel in the hot leg. The Inconel showed heavy subsurface void attack to a depth of 5 mils, and no change in attack in the Inconel was apparent near the nickel insert. evidence.claim_text: An 80\% Mg-20% Li alloy being considered as a crew-compartment shielding material was corrosion tested in water at various temperatures; the weight losses were found to be negligible. In an effort to find a brazing alloy that has good resistance to sodium and to \mathsf{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4} tests of several brazes on type 304 stainless steel T-joints were made. Similar joints of A-nickel brazed with various alloys were also tested in \mathsf{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4} at 1500^{\circ}\mathsf{F} and in sodium hydroxide at 1100 and 1500^{\circ}\mathsf{F} . Additional screening tests were made of carbides of boron, titanium, zirconium, and chromium in static sodium, lithium, and \mathsf{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4} at 1500^{\circ}\mathsf{F} for 100-hr periods. Studies of the corrosion of Inconel, type 316 stainless steel, Hastelloy B, and several special alloys were continued through the use of thermal-convection apparatus.

The data obtained from these loops indicate that reduction of the chromium content of the alloy to 5\% or less greatly reduces corrosion.

evidence.claim_text: The heat transfer coefficients continue to fall about 24% below the general correlation for ordinary fluids, although no corrosion deposits are observed on the tube walls. Some quantitative velocity measurements in a model of an 18-in. core for a reflector-moderated reactor were obtained. A study of the nonuniform electrical heating and nonuniform velocity distributions of fluids flowing in 180-deg bends in high-temperature-differential, high-velocity loops was conducted; an analysis indicated that the wall temperature of the short-radius side of the bend could be significantly higher than the temperature of the long-radius side and thus, perhaps, account for the large differences in corrosion in the two regions. Further heat transfer experiments have been performed with NaF-ZrF _4 -UF _4 (53.5-40-6.5 mole %) flowing in Inconel tubes.

evidence.claim_text: Figure 6.3 shows both the Inconel and the type 316 stainless steel surfaces. The Inconel showed only very light and widely scattered subsurface void formation to a depth of 3 mils, and the depth of attack decreased near the stainless steel joint. Heavy attack extended to a depth of 12 mils and was primarily intergranular in nature. In the cold leg, a well-diffused surface deposit 0.3 mil thick was found. One Inconel loop in which a type 316 stainless steel section 6 in. long had been welded into the upper part of the hot leg was operated with NaF-ZrF _4 -UF _4 (50-46-4 mole %). evidence.claim_text: Metallographic examination of the capsules exposed at 600^{\circ}C revealed light intergranular penetration up to 1 mil in depth. At 1000^{\circ}C the attack was still light, but one isolated case of intergranular penetration to a depth of 18 mils was noted. When the chromium concentration of the melt was plotted against test temperature for the \mathrm{NaZrF}_5, a slight maximum in the curve appeared between 800 and 900^{\circ}C; a similar plot for the \mathrm{UF_4} bearing mixture shows a plateau between 700 to 1000^{\circ}C. However, more chromium appeared in solution in the \mathrm{NaZrF}_5 samples than in the \mathrm{UF_4} bearing mixtures. The tendency toward less corrosion at 1000^{\circ}C than at 800^{\circ}C never appears to be pronounced, but it has been sufficiently persistent in these tests to require explanation. During the past quarter \mathrm{NaZrF}_5 and \mathrm{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4} (53.5-40-6.5 mole \% were tested in type 316 stainless steel under static and nearly isothermal conditions for 100 hr at 100^{\circ}C intervals over the range 600 to 1000^{\circ}C, as a check of the findings with Inconel.

evidence.claim_text: Inconel 600 specimens scanned by IMMA showed a similar pattern of surface inclusions containing B, Na, and F. As yet, the extent of boride formation cannot be quantified in either Hastelloy N or in Inconel 600 by a combination of SSMS and IMMA. Probably, however, reactions (1) and (2) occur to a small extent; boride is deposited at levels not greater than 500~\mathrm{ppm} on Hastelloy N and not exceeding 1000~\mathrm{ppm} on Inconel 600 after four months of contact with molten \mathrm{NaBF_4 - NaF} . The only plausible explanation seems to be that some \mathrm{NaBF_4} remains on (or in) the metal surface despite the washing (5-10 min in boiling water) intended to remove adhering traces of salt.

evidence.claim_text: However, x-ray diffraction of the bulk coating showed it to be over 90\% \mathrm{NaBF}_4 . We also used loop NCL-14 to determine the corrosion properties of pure Cr, Fe, Mo, and Nickel 280 (commercially pure nickel with \mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3} added as a grain refiner) at 600^{\circ}\mathrm{C} in the fluoroborate mixture. As a consequence of the air leaks discussed above, the oxide content of the salt in loop NCL-14 was at an abnormally high level and indicated fairly strongly oxidizing conditions. We initially examined the pure metal specimens after a 234-hr exposure. The chromium and iron specimens were replaced, and a second experiment was conducted for 23 hr. Very little of the chromium specimen remained, and the weight loss of the iron specimen was 40\mathrm{mg/cm}^2 (70 mils/year). Close examination of the chromium specimen showed it to be coated with a black reaction layer about 0.015 in. thick. This black coating has been observed on Hastelloy N specimens exposed to impure \mathrm{NaBF}_4 - 8 mole % NaF in the past,[22] but in these cases it was too thin or too adherent to remove and identify.

evidence.claim_text: The ALPHA pump is driven by a 15-kW (20-hp) variable-speed motor which in turn is supplied by a variable-frequency, variable-voltage M-G set. 861, type DBZ-A, size 101). In previous applications, the ALPHA pump has been driven by V-belts, but this has proved unreliable at speeds above 4000 rpm, particularly with the relatively dense fuel salt mixture, due to upper shaft flexing and vibrations from the belt torque. Therefore, the direct drive was selected for corrosion loops FCL-3 and -4, even though it is more costly because it involves a high-speed motor design and an M-G set for each corrosion loop. The motor and generator are described in Section 3.4, Electrical Systems.
